title: GAF Commercial Roofing Systems in Las Cruces, NM | Commercial Roofing Contractors of Las Cruces

html:
GAF's Commercial Line for Single-Ply and Asphaltic Roofs

GAF is one of the largest roofing manufacturers in the country, and its commercial division covers both single-ply membranes and the asphaltic products the company built its reputation on. That range gives us flexibility when a building's needs don't point cleanly to one membrane category or the other.

EverGuard TPO and PVC

GAF's EverGuard line covers TPO in standard thicknesses along with an EverGuard Extreme TPO product positioned for higher-durability applications, plus a PVC membrane line for buildings with grease or chemical exposure concerns. We evaluate EverGuard against other manufacturers' equivalent products the same way we would any single-ply option — thickness, fleece-back availability, and warranty terms.

On bids where a facilities team wants to compare multiple manufacturers side by side, GAF's EverGuard sheets are one of the standard comparison points because of how widely available the product line is.

Fleece-backed options within EverGuard TPO give added puncture resistance for mechanically attached recover jobs over an irregular existing substrate, similar to what other major manufacturers offer within their own product families.

Asphaltic Heritage — SBS Mod-Bit and BUR

Where GAF stands apart from single-ply-only manufacturers is its deep asphaltic product catalog, including SBS modified bitumen cap and base sheets and traditional built-up roofing components. For buildings where we're recommending mod-bit over single-ply — heavier equipment traffic, hail exposure, or an owner who wants a multi-ply redundant system — GAF's asphaltic lines are a common fit.

This matters locally because plenty of older buildings around downtown and the older commercial corridors already have asphalt-based roofing, and staying within a compatible product family simplifies recover decisions.

Liberty Self-Adhered Cap Sheets

GAF's Liberty line of self-adhered SBS cap sheets is one option we bring up specifically on sites where torch application raises fire risk — buildings backing up to open desert lots or dry vegetation. Self-adhered installation avoids open flame entirely, which matters more here during our driest, windiest stretches of the year.

We also consider Liberty on occupied buildings where tenants are sensitive to torch odor or noise during business hours, since self-adhered application is generally quieter and doesn't carry the same smell as a hot torch application.

Reflective Options Across the Line

GAF offers reflective, white-surfaced options across several of its product categories, from TPO to certain granulated cap sheets. Given the UV and heat load at this elevation, reflectivity is one of the first specs we discuss regardless of which GAF product family a building ends up using.

The reflectivity conversation matters most on buildings with older, poorly insulated roof decks, where a lighter membrane surface can meaningfully reduce the heat load the HVAC system has to fight through a long, hot cooling season.

We factor rooftop equipment and vent placement into that reflectivity discussion too, since some owners prefer a slightly less reflective surface near certain mechanical equipment to reduce glare complaints from adjacent buildings.

  • Membrane category chosen first — single-ply or asphaltic — based on building needs
  • EverGuard thickness and fleece-back options compared against other manufacturers
  • Self-adhered options considered where torch application raises fire risk
  • Reflectivity option selected for this building's specific heat gain profile
  • Warranty terms reviewed against the specific product and installation method
  • Compatibility checked against any existing asphaltic roofing before a recover

Warranty Structure Across Product Categories

GAF's warranty terms vary depending on which product category and installation method are used, and NDL coverage typically requires periodic inspections and manufacturer-approved detailing throughout the roof's service life. We walk owners through the specific warranty document for whichever GAF product is being bid rather than speaking in generalities about coverage.

Because GAF covers both single-ply and asphaltic categories, warranty terms between the two can look quite different even on the same building, which is worth understanding upfront if a property has sections of both roof types under consideration for replacement.

Comparing GAF Against Other Manufacturers

We price GAF alongside Carlisle, Johns Manville, and other manufacturers at bid time rather than defaulting to one brand. GAF's breadth across both single-ply and asphaltic categories makes it a useful comparison point specifically when a building's roofing history includes both membrane types, which happens more often on older properties around the valley than newer construction.

The final recommendation on any bid comes down to what actually fits the building's exposure, existing substrate, and budget — we present the comparison in writing so the decision isn't a matter of taking our word for it.

What Owners Ask About GAF Commercial Products

Does GAF make products for both flat and steep-slope roofs?

Yes, GAF is one of the largest roofing manufacturers in the country and covers both residential steep-slope shingles and commercial low-slope systems, though we work exclusively with the commercial low-slope side.

Is GAF EverGuard comparable to other manufacturers' TPO?

It's built on the same base TPO chemistry as other manufacturers' products, with differences in specific formulation and warranty structure that we compare product-to-product at bid time.

Can GAF's self-adhered cap sheets be used on any roof?

They're suited to most low-slope applications, though we still confirm deck condition and slope requirements before recommending self-adhered versus torch-applied for a specific building.

Does GAF offer both single-ply and asphaltic products under one warranty?

Warranty structures are specific to each product line rather than blanket coverage across categories, so we review the actual warranty documentation for whichever system is being installed.

Why would you recommend GAF over another manufacturer?

It comes down to which specific product line best fits the building's exposure and existing roofing history, not a blanket preference for one manufacturer over another.
